claim: Human T-lymphotropic virus type-I-associated myelopathy / tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP) patients produce Immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies which cross-react with an immunodominant epitope in Tax.

result.claim: Human T-lymphotropic virus type-I-associated myelopathy / tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP) patients produce Immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies which cross-react with an immunodominant epitope in Tax.
result.reasoning: Evidence from paper ID 5476778, "Autoimmunity due to molecular mimicry as a cause of neurological disease", indicates that Immunoglobulin G (IgG) isolated from HAM/TSP patients identifies heterogeneous nuclear ribonuclear protein-A1 (hnRNP-A1) as an autoantigen (Proposition ID: prop_1815). Crucially, these antibodies to hnRNP-A1 were found to cross-react with HTLV-1-tax (Proposition ID: prop_1816). The abstract of the paper further reinforces this by stating, "Immunoglobulin G specifically stained human Betz cells... Antibodies to hnRNP-A1 cross-reacted with HTLV-1-tax, the immune response to which is associated with HAM/TSP" (Chunk ID: chunk_99). While the term "immunodominant epitope" is not explicitly used in the retrieved propositions, the strong cross-reactivity of IgG antibodies from HAM/TSP patients with HTLV-1-tax, and the identification of hnRNP-A1 as an autoantigen, strongly supports the claim that these antibodies cross-react with an epitope in Tax. The paper's focus on molecular mimicry as a cause of neurological disease in HAM/TSP patients further strengthens the link between the immune response and HTLV-1-tax. The paper has 0 citations and an unknown year, which slightly reduces the confidence, but the directness of the propositions is high.
